# Changelog

All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.

The English section comes first, followed by a Japanese translation.
The format is based on [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/), and this project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/spec/v2.0.0.html).

## English

### [Unreleased]

- **Pending changelog fragments live under `changelog.d/unreleased/`** — this section stays empty during ordinary work; see `changelog.d/unreleased/` for the release notes that are waiting to be aggregated.

### [1.22.3] - 2026-05-17

#### Fixed

- **Release verification now waits for uploaded assets before probing downloads** — the release workflow now waits until every release file is visible in the GitHub API as an uploaded, non-empty asset, then verifies the installer assets with ranged GET requests for up to ten minutes so newly created releases do not fail on slow GitHub asset processing or HEAD/CDN mismatches.

### [1.22.2] - 2026-05-17

#### Fixed

- **Release workflow now waits for `sha256sums.txt` to propagate before verifying the install** — the install-verification step polled only the platform tarball, so a release could fail when the CDN had not yet served `sha256sums.txt` (the v1.22.1 release failed with `Failed to download sha256sums.txt ... HTTP 404`). The wait step now polls every asset `install.sh` downloads, so an asset that propagates slowly is waited out instead of failing the release.

### [1.22.1] - 2026-05-17

#### Fixed

- **Release install verification accepts the `--version` build-metadata suffix** — the release workflow's install check compared `cdidx --version` for an exact `cdidx v<tag>` match, so it failed on every release after the build-aware `--version` change (#1550) because the binary now prints a trailing ` (commit <sha>, built <date>, <clean|dirty>)` block. The check is now anchored on the `cdidx v<tag>` prefix, matching the `install.sh` validator.
- **Tagged release builds no longer stamp `--version` as `dirty`** — the RID-targeted `dotnet publish` step rewrites `packages.lock.json` with runtime-specific entries before the build-metadata target re-runs, which made a clean tagged release report `dirty`. The dirty probe now excludes every `packages.lock.json`; genuine lock drift is still caught by the locked-mode restore in CI.

### [1.22.0] - 2026-05-17

#### Added

- **`status` reports indexed HEAD metadata and cross-session drift (#1509)** — `cdidx index` now stamps `indexed_head_sha`, `indexed_head_branch`, and `indexed_head_timestamp` into `codeindex_meta` on a successful run, and `status` (human + `--json` + MCP) surfaces them alongside a new `commits_ahead_of_indexed_head` count, so a stale index ("indexed at commit X, you are now N commits ahead") is detectable across sessions. Non-git workspaces and detached HEAD are handled gracefully (branch omitted, SHA still stamped when available), and stamping is best-effort so a transient `git` failure never fails an otherwise-successful index.
- **Git submodules declared in `.gitmodules` are now indexed even when hosted under SkipDirs-named directories (#1511)** — `FileIndexer` previously inherited a hard SkipDirs block on entries like `vendor/` or `target/`, so a submodule placed under one of those directories (for example `vendor/foo`) was completely invisible to cdidx and produced no cross-module reference edges. The scanner now parses the repository-root `.gitmodules`, overrides SkipDirs along the path to each declared submodule, and walks the submodule itself with its own `.gitignore` / `.cdidxignore` honored. Files directly inside the SkipDirs-named ancestor (or in unrelated siblings of the submodule) remain excluded so the rest of the SkipDirs policy is unchanged. `EvaluatePathFilter` mirrors this passthrough behavior so `--files` / `--commits` update mode stays consistent with a fresh full scan.
- **`cdidx index` now takes a per-database file lock to block concurrent runs (#1514)** — Each `cdidx index` invocation acquires an OS-level lock on `<db>.lock` (using `FileShare.None`, the same primitive `SuggestionStore` already relies on) and writes PID / start-time / host / project metadata to a sibling `<db>.lock.info` file. A second `cdidx index` against the same database now fails fast with `Error: another cdidx index is already running on this database (PID …, started …)` and exits with `CommandExitCodes.DatabaseError` instead of silently racing through schema and data work — which previously could leave SQLite in a corrupted half-and-half state because `busy_timeout=5000` only serializes individual writes. `--dry-run` skips the lock entirely, and `--force` bypasses it with a stderr warning so CI / recovery flows remain possible without manual cleanup. Stale lockfiles from crashed processes recover automatically because the OS releases handles on process death.
- **Forward-compatibility readiness audit on `status` (#1515)** — Every full scan, update, and MCP index now stamps the writing CLI's version into `codeindex_meta` (`cdidx_writer_version`), and `status --json` / MCP status payloads expose three new trust fields: `index_writer_version` (the CLI that last wrote the DB), `index_newer_than_reader` (true when any persisted numeric contract stamp — `metadata_target_version_csharp`, `csharp_symbol_name_contract_version`, `sql_graph_contract_version`, `fold_key_version`, `hotspot_family_version_*` — or unknown `PRAGMA user_version` readiness bits exceed the current binary's compiled maximum), and `index_newer_than_reader_reason` (which specific stamps are newer than the reader). `cdidx status` also prints a human-readable `WARN :` line and `IsStatusDegraded` flips to true in this case, so an older `cdidx` re-opening a DB written by a newer `cdidx` degrades loudly with an audit trail instead of silently flipping readiness bits to false against a workspace that other cdidx clients still treat as authoritative.
- **New `cdidx db --integrity-check` command surfaces SQLite corruption explicitly (#1517)** — previously the only way to confirm a `.cdidx/codeindex.db` was healthy was to wait for a query to crash with a SQLite error, and there was no human- or JSON-friendly probe for CI / oncall workflows. `cdidx db --integrity-check [--db <path>] [--json]` now opens the database read-only, runs `PRAGMA integrity_check`, and reports the result with a stable contract: exit `0` and `ok=true` for a clean database, exit `3` (DatabaseError) with the pragma's diagnostic rows for a corrupted one, exit `2` (NotFound) when the file does not exist (with a hint to run `cdidx index <projectPath>` first), and exit `1` for usage mistakes. Bash / zsh / fish completions and the `--help` output list the new subcommand alongside its `--db`, `--json`, and `--integrity-check` flags. A `--repair` mode is intentionally out of scope for this change — SQLite has no general-purpose repair primitive, so the recommended recovery remains `cdidx index <projectPath> --rebuild`, and the corruption hint now points users there.
- **Honor `NO_COLOR` / `CLICOLOR` / `CLICOLOR_FORCE` for ANSI color output (#1523)** — `cdidx` now disables ANSI color when `NO_COLOR` (any non-empty value, per https://no-color.org) or `CLICOLOR=0` is set, and forces color on when `CLICOLOR_FORCE` is set to a non-empty value other than `0`. `CLICOLOR_FORCE` takes precedence over `NO_COLOR`. CI logs that exported `NO_COLOR=1` no longer accumulate escape sequences in symbol-kind output.
- **`--color {auto,always,never}` flag (#1524)** — adds a global `--color` CLI flag that explicitly opts in to or out of ANSI color, layered on top of the `NO_COLOR` / `CLICOLOR_FORCE` / `CLICOLOR` env handling added in #1523. `--color=always` keeps colored kind labels through pager pipes such as `cdidx symbols Foo | less -R`, and `--color=never` suppresses ANSI even on a TTY. Precedence: `--color` flag > `CLICOLOR_FORCE` > `NO_COLOR` > `CLICOLOR=0` > TTY auto-detect. Invalid `--color` values are rejected with a usage error and a hint listing the supported values. The flag-stripper respects the `--` query-escape sentinel, so `cdidx search -- --color=auto` still treats `--color=auto` as a literal query argument.
- **Stable machine-readable error-code taxonomy on CLI / MCP failures (#1526)** — `cdidx` errors now carry a stable `Exxx_NAME` code so scripts, oncall runbooks, and AI agents can classify failures without substring-matching the human-readable prose. Human stderr output uses the bracketed prefix `Error [E001_DB_NOT_FOUND]: database not found at …` and `--json` envelopes gain an additive optional `error_code` field (omitted when null, so existing consumers see no schema break). Codes wired in this release: `E001_DB_NOT_FOUND` (db file missing), `E002_DB_LOCKED` (SQLite BUSY/LOCKED or per-database file-lock contention), `E003_SCHEMA_TOO_NEW` (reserved — currently surfaced softly via `status --json index_newer_than_reader`), `E004_DB_NOT_WRITABLE`, `E005_DB_INTEGRITY_FAILED` (`PRAGMA integrity_check` rows), `E006_FTS_QUERY_SYNTAX` (raw `--fts` parse error), `E007_TEMP_STORE_EXHAUSTED` (SQLite `SQLITE_FULL`), `E008_DB_ERROR` (generic SQLite fallback), `E009_FEATURE_UNAVAILABLE` (e.g. `--json` on a trimmed/AOT build), `E010_USAGE_ERROR` (argument-parse / conflicting flags), and `E011_DIRECTORY_NOT_FOUND` (project / target directory missing). Once published, codes will not be renamed or reused — retired codes stop emitting but the constants stay in `CommandErrorCodes.cs`. See `DEVELOPER_GUIDE.md` § *Error code taxonomy* for the full table and stability contract.
- **`--json-envelope` wraps `--json` output in a `{ "metadata": {...}, "results": [...] }` object (#1527)** — query commands (`search`, `definition`, `callers`, `callees`, `impact`, `symbols`, `find`, `status`, etc.) accept the new `--json-envelope` flag. When set, the wrapper captures the per-result NDJSON stream into a single JSON document whose `metadata` carries `command`, `cdidx_version`, `elapsed_ms`, `db_path`, `result_count`, `exit_code`, and (when applicable) `query_normalized` and `indexed_at_head_sha`. The legacy flat NDJSON / array output remains the default for one release so existing scripts keep working; the envelope will become the default in the next major version, and the flat form will then be opt-in via `--json-flat`.
- **`impact` truncation now reports `truncated_reason` (#1533)** — when `cdidx impact` (and the `impact_analysis` MCP tool) returns `truncated: true`, the JSON payload now also emits a `truncated_reason` field with one of two values: `user_limit` (the user-supplied `--limit` was reached — raising `--limit` will return more results) or `safety_cap` (an internal per-symbol BFS fetch-iteration cap fired — the input graph is likely pathological and raising `--limit` alone will not help). The MCP summary tail and CLI text-mode footer also surface the reason, giving AI/MCP consumers actionable retry guidance instead of guessing why truncation happened. The field is omitted when `truncated: false`.
- **`impact` can now surface the intermediate symbols on each transitive caller chain via opt-in `--with-paths` (#1536)** — Previously `ImpactResult` exposed only `Depth`, so when BFS converged on the same outer caller through distinct intermediates (e.g. `A → B → foo` and `A → C → foo`) the dedup collapsed those routes into a single row and the "via what" information was lost. Pass `cdidx impact <symbol> --with-paths` (CLI) or `withPaths: true` (MCP `impact_analysis`) to populate a new optional `paths` field on each caller — a list of shortest call chains ordered `[resolvedRoot, intermediate..., callerName]`, with every same-depth convergence surfaced as a distinct entry. JSON omits the field by default to keep payloads compact; a per-row cap (10 paths) is applied with a `paths_truncated` boolean when there are more shortest routes than emitted. Default behavior (without the flag) is unchanged.
- **`batch_query` now returns envelope metadata and per-slot elapsed time (#1537)** — The MCP `batch_query` response gains a top-level `metadata` object exposing `total_elapsed_ms`, `success_count`, and `failure_count`, and every slot in `results` now includes `elapsed_ms` plus a compact `args_summary`. The text content also rolls up the success/failure split (e.g. `Executed 3 queries in 12 ms (1 succeeded, 2 failed).`) so agents can detect partial-failure batches and locate slow inner queries (such as a costly `impact_analysis` hidden inside a 10-query batch) without re-issuing each query individually. The existing `count` and `results` fields are unchanged, so previous consumers continue to work.
- **`validate` flags bare CR (legacy Mac) and three-way line-ending mixes (#1538)** — `FileIndexer.ValidateContent` previously only emitted `mixed_line_endings` for files that contained both CRLF and LF, and `BuildRecordWithRawBytes`'s `"\r\n" → "\n"` then `"\r" → "\n"` normalization silently flattened bare CR (legacy Mac) endings, leaving line-counting / regex assumptions unverified for SVN-imported sources. Validation now scans raw bytes for CR, CRLF, and LF independently and emits a new `cr_only_line_endings` issue when only bare CR is present and a `mixed_line_endings_three_way` issue when all three coexist; two-way CR + (CRLF | LF) mixes still surface as `mixed_line_endings` with a descriptive message. The new kinds appear in `cdidx validate` / `--kind` filters and the MCP `validate` tool description.
- **Golden-file regression fixtures for the CLI `--json` output contracts (#1548)** — added `JsonOutputSnapshotTests` plus a `JsonOutputSnapshotHelper` that runs `status` / `search` / `references` / `impact` / `excerpt` against deterministic in-memory fixtures, normalizes volatile fields (timestamps, absolute paths, commit SHAs, FTS5 scores, version strings), and diffs the result against checked-in golden files under `tests/CodeIndex.Tests/golden/`. Renames, removals, reordered arrays, or new keys now fail the snapshot, so any `--json` shape change is forced to land alongside an intentional golden update. The update procedure (set `UPDATE_SNAPSHOTS=1` and re-run only the snapshot tests, then review the diff) is documented in `TESTING_GUIDE.md`.
- **Opt-in JSONL metrics emission for CLI + MCP (#1549)** — `cdidx` now accepts a `--metrics <path>` flag (or the `CDIDX_METRICS` env var) and appends one JSON-lines record per CLI command and per MCP tool call to that path. Each record carries `timestamp`, `tool`, `source` (`cli` / `mcp`), `elapsed_ms`, `exit_code`, and — when known — `language`, so latency regressions or throughput drops can be detected from logs alone without re-running queries. Metrics emission is best-effort: a missing directory, an unwritable path, or any IO failure is swallowed silently and never breaks the underlying command. Optional fields (`bytes_read`, `bytes_written`, `wal_checkpoint_ms`, `files_indexed`, `error`) are omitted when null so future consumers can grow new columns without breaking older parsers.
- **New `cdidx report --output <path>` command builds a redacted bug-report bundle (#1552)** — until now, asking a user "please share what cdidx version, OS, and DB schema you are on" required them to gather and redact several pieces by hand, and there was no commonly-shaped artifact to attach to a GitHub issue. `cdidx report --output report.tgz` now emits a deterministic `.tar.gz` containing `metadata.json` (cdidx version, .NET runtime, OS / process architecture, generated-at UTC), `version.txt`, `env.txt`, `schema.txt` (SQLite table names and integer row counts only — no user content), and a `log/stderr-recent.log` tail of the cdidx lifecycle log. By default the tail's `cwd=` and `args=` lines are replaced with `[redacted]` so working-directory paths and literal query strings never leave the user's machine; `--include-args` is an explicit opt-in for trusted recipients, and `--no-log` omits the log entirely. Supporting flags `--db <path>` (override schema source), `--log-lines <n>` (tail size, default 200), and `--json` (stable summary envelope including `output_path`, `version`, `files`, `schema_tables`, `log_lines_included`, `log_included`, `db_included`, `db_path`) round out the contract. Exit codes follow the existing convention: `0` on success, `1` for usage errors, `3` when the bundle cannot be written. The command never reads indexed source content — only table names and row counts — so the bundle is safe to attach to public issues for repos where the source itself is sensitive.
- **Releases now ship a CycloneDX SBOM (`cdidx.sbom.cdx.json`) (#1553)** — every `v*` GitHub release publishes a CycloneDX 1.x JSON Software Bill of Materials alongside the per-platform `CodeIndex-<rid>.tar.gz` / `.zip` binaries and the existing `sha256sums.txt`. The SBOM is generated once per release on the `linux-x64` lane via the pinned `dotnet CycloneDX` global tool against `src/CodeIndex/CodeIndex.csproj` with `--exclude-test-projects`, uploaded as a `CodeIndex-sbom` build artifact, copied into `release-files/` so `sha256sums.txt` covers its hash, and shipped as a release asset. The SBOM enumerates every NuGet dependency (including the bundled `Microsoft.Data.Sqlite` / `SQLitePCLRaw` graph and the native `e_sqlite3` asset), so enterprise consumers can run SOC2 / FedRAMP-style compliance reviews and supply-chain scanners (Snyk, Trivy, Grype) without re-deriving the dependency graph from the .NET-specific `.deps.json`. `README.md` adds a "Verifying releases" section that documents the SBOM location and a `sha256sum --check` quick-verify command, and `DEVELOPER_GUIDE.md` adds the SBOM to the release "moving parts" table.
- **`api_version` field on top-level CLI/MCP JSON DTOs (#1555)** — every top-level CLI/MCP JSON output DTO (`StatusResult`, `RepoMapResult`, `SymbolAnalysisResult`, `ImpactAnalysisResult`, `OutlineResult`, `FileExcerptResult`, `CompactSearchResult`, `SymbolResult`, `DefinitionResult`, `UnusedSymbolResult`, `ReferenceResult`, `CallerResult`, `CalleeResult`, `FileResult`, `FileFindResult`) now carries an `api_version` string field stamped from the new public `JsonOutputContract.ApiVersion` constant, and `--json-envelope` mirrors the same value on its `metadata` block. This describes the JSON output contract, not the cdidx binary version, so strict downstream consumers can pin against a known shape and detect breaking renames/removals/type-changes (the version is bumped only on those, not on additive fields).
- **Optional HTTP transport for the MCP server (#1558)** — `cdidx mcp` now accepts `--transport stdio|http` (default `stdio`) plus `--http-listen <host:port>` (default `127.0.0.1:38080`). The HTTP transport accepts one JSON-RPC frame per POST and returns the matching response in the same HTTP body (notifications return `204 No Content`), keeping the request/response ordering invariant the existing stdio loop relies on. The listener refuses to bind to non-loopback hosts unless a shared secret is supplied via the `CDIDX_MCP_HTTP_TOKEN` environment variable, in which case every request must carry a matching `Authorization: Bearer ...` header. Stdio behavior is byte-for-byte unchanged, so existing clients continue to work without modification.
- **MCP server authentication framework with optional `CDIDX_MCP_AUTH_TOKEN` (#1559)** — Adds an `IMcpAuthenticator` middleware invoked once per JSON-RPC request before method dispatch, producing an `McpCallerIdentity` (source + subject) the future audit log (#1562) and any networked transports can consume. The default `LocalStdioAuthenticator` is permissive and preserves the historical stdio behaviour, so existing clients (Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf) keep working without changes. Setting the `CDIDX_MCP_AUTH_TOKEN` environment variable to a non-whitespace value swaps in `TokenMcpAuthenticator`, which enforces a constant-time (`CryptographicOperations.FixedTimeEquals`) match against `params.auth.token` on every responded request — `initialize`, `tools/list`, `tools/call`, and `ping` — and rejects mismatches with a uniform JSON-RPC `-32001 "Unauthorized"` response so the wire body cannot distinguish missing-from-wrong tokens. Notifications (`notifications/initialized`, `notifications/cancelled`) still short-circuit before the auth gate because they produce no response and cannot signal an error code. The full failure reason is written to stderr via the new `BuildAuthFailureLog` helper for local diagnostics; the wire response stays sanitized per #1530.
- **Opt-in per-(tool, caller) rate limiting for MCP tool calls (#1560)** — `cdidx mcp` now supports a token-bucket throttle keyed by `(tool, caller)` so a misbehaving client cannot exhaust CPU or memory by spamming `batch_query` carrying multiple `search` calls. Disabled by default so single-user stdio sessions are unaffected; enable by setting `CDIDX_MCP_RATE_LIMIT_RPS` (tokens per second) and optionally `CDIDX_MCP_RATE_LIMIT_BURST` (bucket capacity, default = `max(rps, 1)`) on the server process. Caller identity is captured from the `clientInfo.name` (and `version` when present) of the `initialize` request, falling back to `"unknown"`. Over-quota calls return JSON-RPC `-32000` with `data.error_category = "rate_limited"`, `data.tool`, `data.caller`, and `data.retry_after_ms` so clients can back off. Inside `batch_query`, each inner slot is also checked against the inner-tool bucket and over-quota slots surface `error_category` + `retry_after_ms` in the per-slot result.
- **Per-deployment MCP tool enablement gate (#1561)** — operators can now restrict which MCP tools `cdidx mcp` advertises and dispatches. Set `CDIDX_MCP_TOOLS_ALLOW` to a comma-separated allowlist (e.g. `CDIDX_MCP_TOOLS_ALLOW=search,references,callers`) to pin the visible/callable surface for a read-only or scoped session, or set `CDIDX_MCP_TOOLS_DENY` (e.g. `CDIDX_MCP_TOOLS_DENY=index,backfill_fold`) to remove individual tools from the default-all-enabled set. Allow wins over deny when both are set. `tools/list` only advertises enabled tools, the `initialize` instructions string no longer recommends disabled tools, and a top-level `tools/call` on a disabled known tool returns the structured `-32601 Tool not enabled: <name>` JSON-RPC error so AI clients can branch on a code instead of parsing prose. The `batch_query` envelope still succeeds, but each disabled-tool slot carries a `code: -32601` field alongside the `error` string for the same branching contract. Truly unknown tool names still surface as the existing `-32602 Unknown tool`, keeping operator-disabled tools distinguishable from typos. Unknown names in either env var are filtered against the known tool set; a typo'd allowlist that names only unknown tools intentionally exposes nothing rather than silently disabling the gate.
- **Opt-in structured audit log for MCP tool invocations (#1562)** — `cdidx mcp` now accepts `--audit-log <path>` (with companion `--audit-log-include-values` and `--audit-log-max-bytes <n>` flags) to emit one JSONL record per `tools/call`. Each record captures the timestamp, MCP tool name, caller (`initialize.clientInfo.{name,version}`), request id, argument *keys* and per-key length sketch, observed `result_count`, wall-clock `elapsed_ms`, and an `error_code` that maps to `0` on success, `1` for tool errors, or the verbatim JSON-RPC error code for protocol failures. Argument *values* are off by default and gated behind `--audit-log-include-values` because cdidx queries can carry source snippets or secret-shaped strings. Malformed `tools/call` requests (missing tool name), unknown tools, and operator-disabled tools (#1561 deny-list) are all audited so a misbehaving client cannot hide its activity by varying the request shape and so deny-list policy is auditable after the fact. The destination is probe-opened at construction so misconfigured paths (existing directory, denied parent) fail loudly before the server claims auditing is on; misconfigured `--db --audit-log` argument ordering preserves the `--db` value instead of consuming it as an audit flag; and every fresh `initialize` reseats caller identity so a reconnect that omits `clientInfo` cannot inherit the previous client's name on subsequent records. The log file is opened append-only, rotated through `<path>.1` / `<path>.2` at the configured size cap (default 50 MiB, minimum 4 KiB), and capped at three slots total so a slow drain of the log cannot fill the disk. Emission is best-effort once construction succeeds: any later IO failure is swallowed so audit writing never breaks the underlying tool call. Compliance reviewers can now answer "who called which tool with what shape of arguments and when did it fail?" without re-running the index.
- **`--palette` flag and `CDIDX_COLOR_PALETTE` env var pick the ANSI palette; `basic` no longer emits unreadable `\x1b[90m` dim (#1569)** — `ColorizeKind` previously hard-coded 8 ANSI colors plus `\x1b[90m` (bright-black / dim) without inspecting terminal capability, so SSH and CI terminals that render dim as invisible silently lost the `namespace` and `import` labels, and truecolor-capable terminals saw the same coarse 16-color rendering. `cdidx` now resolves a palette via `--palette basic|256|truecolor` (with aliases `8`/`16`/`ansi`, `color256`/`8bit`, `24bit`/`rgb`) and a matching `CDIDX_COLOR_PALETTE` environment variable, falling back to `COLORTERM=truecolor|24bit` and `TERM=*256color*` auto-detection and finally to the safer `basic` palette. The basic palette is now strictly 8-color (SGR 30–37): `namespace` and `import` use white (`\x1b[37m`) instead of `\x1b[90m`, so minimal terminals stay readable. The 256-color and truecolor palettes emit higher-contrast `\x1b[38;5;Nm` / `\x1b[38;2;R;G;Bm` codes on capable terminals. `NO_COLOR` / `--color=never` continue to suppress ANSI escapes across every palette.
- **Project-local configuration via `.cdidxrc.json` (#1571)** — `cdidx` now walks upward from the current working directory (like `.editorconfig`) for a `.cdidxrc.json` file and materializes recognized keys as process environment variables on startup so every existing env-var consumer picks them up unchanged. Precedence is CLI flag > environment variable > config file > built-in default: the loader only writes a value when the matching env var is not already set, so anything the user already exported in a shell or CI still wins. The schema is strict (snake_case keys; unknown keys are a hard usage error), accepts JSON5 line comments and trailing commas, and recognizes `debug`, `metrics_path`, `disable_persistent_log`, `global_tool_log_dir`, plus nested `mcp.tools.allow` / `mcp.tools.deny` and `mcp.rate_limit.rps` / `mcp.rate_limit.burst`. Secrets (`CDIDX_GITHUB_TOKEN`, `CDIDX_MCP_AUTH_TOKEN`, `CDIDX_MCP_HTTP_TOKEN`) are intentionally env-only so tokens never get checked into version control. A malformed file fails fast with the path and offending field, and `CDIDX_DISABLE_CONFIG_FILE=1` bypasses the lookup entirely.
- **Property-based tests for parser-heavy paths (#1572)** — added an FsCheck.Xunit test suite (`PropertyBasedParserTests`) that asserts universal invariants on the parser surfaces called out in the issue: `ArgHelper.WantsHelp` never throws on arbitrary `string[]`, `ProgramRunner.IsProjectPathArg` never throws on any non-null token, `FileIndexer.NormalizePathSeparators` is idempotent under double application, and the literal-safe FTS5 sanitizer (`DbReader.SanitizeFtsQuery`) always emits a query that an in-memory FTS5 virtual table actually accepts. The sanitizer's visibility was relaxed from `private` to `internal` so the property test can reach it across the existing `InternalsVisibleTo` boundary.
- **`cdidx index --watch` keeps the index live by re-running incremental updates as files change (#1576)** — after the initial scan completes, the command stays running and uses `FileSystemWatcher` (FSEvents on macOS, inotify on Linux, ReadDirectoryChangesW on Windows) to detect creates, edits, renames, and deletes anywhere under the project root. Events are coalesced through a debounce window (`--debounce <ms>`, default 500 ms) so a burst of saves becomes one batched `--files` update; gitignore / `.cdidxignore` rules and SkipDirs are honored, the per-DB `IndexLock` is released between batches so other cdidx commands can still query, and watcher-buffer overflow falls back to a full incremental rescan rather than silently dropping changes. The loop emits `status: "watching" / "updated" / "rescanned" / "overflow" / "stopped"` JSON events when `--json` is set (otherwise a human-readable `[watch] …` line on stderr per batch) and exits cleanly on Ctrl+C / SIGTERM. The flag rejects `--commits`, `--files`, and `--dry-run` with an actionable hint because watch already drives continuous incremental updates.
- **Structured error envelope and server-defined codes for the MCP server (#1581)** — `cdidx mcp` now returns a canonical `data` envelope (`category`, `suggestion`, `retry_safe`) on every JSON-RPC `error` object and every MCP tool-result error (`isError: true`), so AI clients can branch on a stable machine-readable category instead of parsing the human `message` string. Server-defined JSON-RPC codes are allocated within the reserved `-32000..-32099` range: `-32000 rate_limited`, `-32001 permission_denied`, `-32010 index_missing`, `-32011 index_stale`, `-32012 index_corrupted`, `-32015 request_cancelled`. Standard JSON-RPC codes (`-32700` / `-32600..-32603`) keep their semantics with new categories layered on top (`parse_error`, `invalid_request`, `message_too_large`, `method_not_found`, `tool_unknown`, `tool_disabled`, `missing_parameter`, `invalid_argument`, `internal_error`). Unhandled exceptions inside `tools/call` go through `McpErrorEnvelope.ClassifyException`, which maps `SqliteException` to `index_stale` (missing table/column) or `index_corrupted` (malformed / not-a-database / encrypted), `OperationCanceledException` to `request_cancelled`, and everything else to `internal_error`; the raw exception message is never leaked on the wire (per #1530 sanitization). Legacy fields on rate-limited responses (`error_category`, `tool`, `caller`, `retry_after_ms` from #1560) are preserved alongside the new canonical envelope so existing clients keep working. The full code/category map is documented in `DEVELOPER_GUIDE.md`.
- **Read commands can emit SQL profiles for slow-query diagnosis (#1643)** — `--profile` now appends SQL timing, row-count, and `EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN` JSON after normal read-command output, and `--slow-query-ms <n>` logs profiled SQL statements that meet the threshold.
- **Windows ARM64 release artifacts are now published (#1648)** — the release workflow now builds and uploads `CodeIndex-win-arm64.zip`, and the platform support documentation and installer RID guidance list `win-arm64` as an official release asset.
- **Added first-class git pre-commit hook integration (#1854)** — `cdidx hooks install|status|uninstall` can manage a repository pre-commit hook that runs `cdidx index . --quiet`, chains an existing hook, and keeps indexing errors visible.
- **`hotspots` now exposes explicit grouping metadata and selection (#1866)** — `hotspots --group-by=symbol|file|statement` and MCP `symbol_hotspots` `groupBy` let callers choose the grouping unit, while JSON responses include `grouped_by`; SQL scopes keep their existing statement-oriented default and non-SQL scopes default to symbol grouping.
- **Suggestion records now capture agent attribution metadata (#1873)** — `suggest_improvement` records the MCP client name/version, an opaque cdidx session id, the cdidx version, and optional natural-language invocation context so accumulated suggestions can be correlated by agent and session.
- **Suggestion records now track lifecycle metadata (#1874)** — local suggestion records now store lifecycle status plus upstream issue URL/number and sync timestamps, and legacy `submitted_to_github` records are migrated on read.
- **`cdidx suggestions` exposes local suggestion history (#1876)** — users can now list, inspect, filter, and export suggestions stored beside `.cdidx/codeindex.db`, including JSON output for scripts and markdown export for team triage.
- **Suggestion history can now be queried without loading the whole store first (#1877)** — `SuggestionStore` adds streaming readers for lifecycle status, creation time, category, language, and stored-order pages.
- **Branch-switch partial indexing can now target changed refs (#2239)** — `cdidx index <projectPath> --changed-between <old-ref> <new-ref>` updates only files changed between two git refs and includes both old and new rename paths so stale indexed paths can be purged.

#### Changed

- **MCP sessions reuse a single SQLite connection across tool calls (#1494)** — `McpServer` now opens a per-session `DbContext` on the first tool invocation and reuses it for every subsequent call, so each tool request no longer pays the cost of reopening the SQLite connection, reapplying pragmas, re-registering SQL functions, and rerunning `TryMigrateForRead` round trips. `cdidx mcp` and `McpServer` are now `IDisposable`; in long-running sessions a small `excerpt` or `status` call is now dominated by the actual query work instead of connection setup.
- **Missing-value CLI errors now print a per-flag `Hint:` line (#1507)** — running a `cdidx` query command with a value-taking flag but no value (e.g. `cdidx search --limit`) now prints both the original `Error: --limit requires a value.` line and a follow-up `Hint:` line that names the expected value type or range and gives a concrete example (`Hint: pass a positive integer, e.g. --limit 20 (default 20).`). Hints are sourced from a single per-flag metadata table inside `QueryCommandRunner`, so `--limit`, `--top`, `--db`, `--lang`, `--query`, `--kind`, `--depth`, `--path`, `--exclude-path`, `--since`, `--start`, `--end`, `--before`, `--after`, `--focus-line`, `--focus-column`, `--focus-length`, `--name`, `--snippet-lines`, and `--max-line-width` all surface consistent guidance across `search`, `find`, `symbols`, `references`, `callers`, `callees`, `definition`, `impact`, `excerpt`, `files`, `hotspots`, `unused`, and other query commands. Users no longer have to consult `--help` to recover from a trivially missed value.
- **`search` is now strict by default for every script; FTS5 prefix expansion is opt-in via `--prefix` or trailing `*` (#1519)** — The literal-safe `search` path no longer auto-upgrades CJK (or any other) tokens to an FTS5 prefix phrase. Searching `cdidx search 計算` against content containing `計算する` / `計算機` / `計算結果` now matches only the indexed token `計算` and stops widening into longer neighboring tokens. To opt into prefix expansion, append `*` to a single token (`cdidx search 計算*` promotes that one token) or pass the new `--prefix` flag / MCP `prefix` argument to widen every token in the query. `--prefix` cannot be combined with `--exact` / `--exact-substring` (those bypass FTS5 entirely) and is rejected with a usage-error hint. The previous unconditional CJK prefix promotion existed because the default FTS5 unicode61 tokenizer keeps a run of adjacent CJK codepoints as a single token, but that silently widened exact CJK identifier lookups and broke relevance for users hunting precise names; the new opt-in model preserves the broad-match capability while restoring strict semantics by default.
- **`impact_analysis` `maxDepth` cap raised to 50 and clamping is now surfaced instead of silent (#1534)** — the MCP `impact_analysis` tool previously clamped `maxDepth` to `[0, 10]` without telling the caller, so a request for `maxDepth: 100` was silently downgraded to `10` and deep monorepo caller chains (DI container → factory → service → handler → business logic, ≥5 hops) underreported blast radius. The server-side cap is now `50`, and when the user-supplied value exceeds it the response payload adds a `warnings` array (`"maxDepth was clamped from 100 to 50 (server cap is [0, 50])."`) plus a `max_depth_requested` field carrying the original request, and the human-readable `content` text appends the same warning. The cap is documented in the MCP tool schema (`maximum: 50`, plus a description note about the `warnings` / `max_depth_requested` contract) so agents can see the bound before issuing a request. Default `maxDepth` and the BFS bound semantics are unchanged; only the upper cap and visibility behaviour change.
- **`cdidx --version` now stamps commit SHA, build date, and clean/dirty flag so dev builds are distinguishable from tagged releases (#1550)** — The human output is one line: `cdidx v<ver> (commit <sha>, built <yyyy-mm-dd>, <clean|dirty>)`. The new `cdidx --version --json` form emits `{name, version, commit, build_date, dirty}` for support tooling and bug-report templates. Metadata comes from an MSBuild target (`StampCdidxBuildMetadata`) that runs `git rev-parse --short=7 HEAD` and `git diff-index --quiet HEAD --` at build time and writes the values into `AssemblyMetadataAttribute`s, so no new runtime dependencies are introduced and reflection-trim builds keep working through the existing `CliJsonSerializerContext`. When the stamp is absent (legacy assemblies, mocked test binaries) `FormatVersionLine` falls back to bare `cdidx v<ver>`, preserving the previous shape. The `install.sh` reinstall validator was tightened to accept either the bare form or the parenthesised metadata suffix only — trailing diagnostic text without parens is still rejected so token-enumeration drift in `--version` cannot hide install regressions.
- **MCP `protocolVersion` negotiation in `initialize` (#1554)** — `cdidx mcp` no longer ignores the `protocolVersion` field that the client sends in the `initialize` request. The server now maintains a `SupportedProtocolVersions` set (`2025-03-26`, `2024-11-05`) and echoes the client's requested version when it is supported, falls back to the newest server version when the client omits or sends a non-string value, and rejects unsupported versions with a structured JSON-RPC `-32602` error whose `error.data` carries `requestedVersion` and `supportedVersions`. This means future MCP spec bumps surface as a clear handshake failure with actionable guidance instead of a silent wire-format mismatch.
- **`DbReader` no longer re-registers SQL user functions on every construction (#1564)** — the ~20 SQL user functions (`sql_leaf_name`, `sql_normalize_name_folded`, `sql_resolve_reference_name`, etc.) are already registered once per connection by `DbContext`, so dropping the redundant call from the `DbReader` constructor removes wasted CPU on hot MCP/CLI paths that build a short-lived reader per request. Constructing a `DbReader` from a warm `DbContext.Connection` is now decoupled from the SQL function-table size.
- **`DbReader` reuses a connection-scoped schema cache instead of re-running `PRAGMA table_info` / `PRAGMA index_list` per construction (#1565)** — the new `DbSchemaCache` (attached to `DbContext.SchemaCache`) memoizes the `files` / `symbols` / `symbol_references` column sets, the `symbols` / `symbol_references` index sets, and `sqlite_master` table-existence checks. The MCP `WithDbReader` hot path and the CLI `WithDb` reader path now route through the cache, so each subsequent tool call pays the schema scan once per session instead of once per `DbReader`. Every lookup first consults `PRAGMA schema_version`, so DDL run through a separate connection — for example, a long-running MCP server while `cdidx index` rebuilds the same DB out of band — automatically invalidates the cache before the next reader is served and no server restart is required. `DbContext.RefreshSchemaCache()` remains exposed for callers that want to force an invalidation; `InitializeSchema`, `TryMigrateForRead`, and `DropAll` invalidate the cache automatically. The legacy `(SqliteConnection, bool)` constructor still falls back to inline PRAGMA queries so write-side signal readers that hand in a raw connection continue to work unchanged.
- **Reuse prepared statements on hot per-file write paths (#1566)** — `DbWriter` now leases per-file `SqliteCommand` instances from a bounded LRU `PreparedCommandCache` shared with its owning `DbContext`, so per-file lookups (`GetUnchangedFileId`, `HasFileAtPath`, `CleanExistingFileData`), the `UpsertFile` INSERT…ON CONFLICT, the four `DeleteFileData` deletes, and the `DeleteFileByPath` delete all hit one parsed/planned statement per SQL text instead of constructing and disposing a fresh command per call. Indexing a 10k-file project previously paid ~100k extra parse/plan cycles on these hot paths; with this change each statement is parsed once per session and rebinds parameter values for subsequent calls. Cached commands are re-pointed at the writer's active `SqliteTransaction` on every lease so reuse remains safe across nested savepoints and transaction boundaries. The cache is opt-in via the new `DbWriter(DbContext)` constructor — the legacy `DbWriter(SqliteConnection)` ctor still works without caching, preserving every existing test and external call site.
- **CLI easter-egg messages now respect a user-facing language setting (#1568)** — bilingual user-facing strings in `ConsoleUi.PrintEasterEggMessage` are no longer inlined as raw English+Japanese pairs. The new `UiMessages` catalog (in `src/CodeIndex/Cli/UiI18n.cs`) co-locates each translation, and `UiLanguageResolver` picks the active language with precedence `CDIDX_LANG` env (`en` / `ja` / `both` and common aliases) > `CultureInfo.CurrentUICulture` (`ja-*` → Japanese) > English fallback. **Default-output change:** the easter-egg banners (`cdidx --sushi`, `--coffee`, `--ramen`, `--wine`, `--beer`, `--matcha`, `--whisky`) previously always printed both an English line and a Japanese line; they now print only the resolver-selected language. Set `CDIDX_LANG=both` (or `bilingual`) to restore the previous always-bilingual output. This is the first step in extracting all user-facing strings from `ConsoleUi` (and, later, `ProgramRunner`) into a single i18n catalog so new translations no longer require editing every call site.
- **CLI flags now live in a single schema instead of being duplicated across help text, parser allowlists, and shell completion templates (#1570)** — `CliFlagSchema.cs` is the new source of truth: each flag declares its `Commands` (parser + completion) and `AlsoAcceptedBy` (parser-only, hidden from completion, e.g. `--exact-name` on `search`). `QueryCommandRunner` now drives every `TryWriteUnsupportedOptionError` allowlist and `ValidateFindArgs` from this schema, and `ConsoleUi` generates the bash / zsh / fish completion scripts from it too. Adding a new flag is now a one-row change instead of four hand-synchronized edits. `CliFlagSchemaTests` guards the SSoT contract (parity between schema and `ConsoleUi.Commands`, no flag references an unknown subcommand, completion scripts contain every schema flag and nothing extra, parser-vs-completion drift is intentional). Side effects: per-branch `--max-line-width` tooltips (previously varied between `snippets` / `contexts` / `excerpts`) now use a single canonical "payloads" wording.
- **"Did you mean" suggestions now cover flags and enum-style values, not just subcommands (#1582)** — the Damerau-Levenshtein suggester previously used only by the subcommand router is now exposed as a generic `ConsoleUi.FindClosestMatch` / `FindClosestMatches` helper and applied to (a) unknown long flags on `index`, `backfill-fold`, `find`, and every `search` / graph query family path that already reports "is not supported" — both the separated `--paht src/**` and inline `--paht=src/**` forms surface the same suggestion, (b) `--lang` values that do not match the indexed language set (with a fallback to `ReferenceExtractor.GetSupportedLanguages()` so typos like `--lang csarp` still suggest `--lang csharp` against an empty/fresh index), and (c) `--kind` and graph reference-kind values, including `validate --kind` typos which are now matched against the full file-issue-kind allowlist instead of only kinds present in the index. A typo like `cdidx index --rebild` now prints `Did you mean: --rebuild?` to stderr in addition to the existing warning, `cdidx search foo --lang csarp` now prints `Did you mean: --lang csharp?`, and `cdidx validate --kind replacement_chra` now prints `Did you mean: --kind replacement_char?` instead of silently treating the typo as a clean repo. The same length-aware threshold (`<=4 chars: 1`, `<=10: 2`, else `3`) is reused, so off-by-many tokens like `--zzzzzzzz` still print no suggestion. MCP responses for unknown enumerated values (currently `suggest_improvement`'s `category` argument) now also carry a structured `result.data.similar_values: [...]` array next to the human-readable "Did you mean: ..." text so MCP clients can offer one-tap recovery without parsing the message string.
- **`status --check` now reports failed freshness and readiness checks (#1852)** — failed checks are surfaced as stderr diagnostics for shell use, as `failed_checks` in JSON output, and can be scoped with `--check=fold,graph,hotspot,csharp` and related status scopes.
- **Human CLI file-size output now uses binary units (#1855)** — `files` and `map` text output render sizes as `KiB` / `MiB` / `GiB` while `--bytes` preserves raw byte counts and `--json` keeps raw integer bytes for machines.
- **Search ranking now prefers more visible symbol matches (#1868)** — `search` ranks public/exported symbol-bearing hits ahead of protected, internal, and private matches, exposes `visibility` in JSON results, shows non-public visibility in human output, and supports `--no-visibility-rank` for legacy ordering.
- **`hotspots` now ranks lower-signal call-graph edges below real invocations (#1875)** — hotspot ordering uses a weighted invocation score so `subscribe` edges no longer outrank fewer direct `call` / `instantiate` sites solely by raw count, while human and JSON output still expose the raw reference count.
- **Call-graph reference kinds now use a consistent logical taxonomy (#1880)** — default `callers` / `callees` output reports `call` and `instantiate` as `invoke`, event subscription rows as `event`, and adds `--raw-kinds` for users who need raw extractor labels.
- **SQLite WAL durability settings are now explicit (#1925)** — writable indexes now use `synchronous=NORMAL`, pin `wal_autocheckpoint=1000`, run passive checkpoints after outer write commits, and expose the resolved values in `status --json` as `db_pragma_settings`.

#### Fixed

- **C# static member qualifiers are now indexed as class references (#1404)** — `Class.Member(...)` and `Class.Field` usages now create reference edges to the class qualifier, so `references`, `callers`, `impact`, and `unused` no longer miss classes used only through static member access.
- **C# type-reference extraction now reuses line-ordered container lookup state (#1433)** — reference extraction avoids rewalking every candidate container for repeated source-ordered lookup requests, reducing superlinear indexing cost on large generated files with many type-position tokens.
- **Razor control directive scanning no longer repeatedly walks each line (#1434)** — `.cshtml` and `.razor` reference extraction now finds control directives in a single forward pass per line, reducing indexing overhead on Razor-heavy projects with many `@if`, `@foreach`, and related blocks.
- **PHP docblock reference extraction now reuses the enclosing container per docblock (#1435)** — PHP docblock type tags no longer repeat the same container-tree walk for every tag in a block, reducing indexing overhead for verbose PHP comments.
- **Python type-reference extraction now reuses definition container lookups (#1436)** — Python class-base and function annotation reference extraction no longer repeatedly scans the full symbol list for same-line class/function containers.
- **Exact graph lookups use composite reference indexes (#1437)** — `references`, `callers`, and `callees` now create composite `symbol_references` indexes for case-insensitive and Unicode-folded exact lookups paired with reference kind or source file filtering, including callee container-name lookups, reducing per-row filtering on high-fan-in symbols.
- **C# required property signatures are covered by regression tests (#1456)** — `public required` properties now have explicit coverage proving `required` and `init` remain in extracted signatures.
- **MCP server stdio loop now uses `ConfigureAwait(false)` (#1493)** — every `await` in the JSON-RPC read/write loop now opts out of capturing the current `SynchronizationContext`, removing per-call overhead in long-running MCP sessions and avoiding latent deadlocks or unintended serialization when a host installs a non-default `SynchronizationContext`.
- **`GitHelper` no longer uses sync-over-async on git stderr (#1497)** — `GetChangedFilesFromCommit` and `TryRunGit` previously called `process.StandardError.ReadToEndAsync()` and then `errorTask.GetAwaiter().GetResult()`, which risked thread-pool starvation when the same helpers were reached from MCP request handlers via `WorkspaceMetadataEnricher`. Both call sites now drain stdout and stderr concurrently via `Process.BeginOutputReadLine` / `BeginErrorReadLine`, a purely synchronous pattern that preserves the original deadlock-resistance against full stderr pipe buffers without blocking on a `Task`.
- **CSS reference extractor now emits `#id` selector references (#1498)** — `body #header { ... }` now produces a `reference` edge to `#header`, so `callers` / `impact` follow CSS ID selectors instead of treating defined IDs as unused. Hex color literals such as `#fff` and `#abc123` in property values are still ignored.
- **Shell extractor now follows commands inside `$(...)` and backtick substitutions (#1499)** — `ShellReferenceExtractor` re-scans the raw line for `$(...)` command substitution and `` `...` `` backtick substitution spans (including nested `$(outer $(inner))` forms), so call sites such as `result=$(helper)` and ``output=`helper arg` `` now emit `call`-kind reference edges. `callers helper` and `impact` no longer underreport helpers composed via substitution. Single-quoted look-alikes (`'$(helper)'`) remain ignored.
- **CSS extractor now emits `@import` and SCSS `@include` edges (#1501)** — `@import "..."` / `@import url(...)` now produce `import`-kind reference edges to the imported stylesheet, and SCSS `@include name(args)` produces a `call`-kind edge to the mixin definition, so cross-stylesheet dependencies surface in `references` / `impact` and SCSS mixins no longer appear as zero-usage symbols.
- **Terraform dotted queries (`var.X`, `local.Y`, `module.Z`, `data.TYPE.X`) now resolve to references (#1502)** — references emitted by the Terraform extractor are stored under the bare symbol name (e.g. `instances`), so user-facing queries copied straight from HCL such as `cdidx references var.instances` previously returned no results. The query layer now strips Terraform's dotted prefixes before searching, matching the behavior already used for JavaScript CommonJS dotted exports.
- **Numeric CLI flags now enforce per-flag upper bounds (#1503)** — `--limit` / `--top` (≤ 10000), `--snippet-lines` (≤ 20, matching the MCP `search` tool schema), `--depth` (≤ 64), `--before` / `--after` (≤ 1000), `--start` / `--end` / `--focus-line` (≤ 10000000), `--focus-column` / `--focus-length` (≤ 100000) now reject values above the documented maximum with `Error: <flag> must be less than or equal to <max>, got '<value>'.` instead of silently parsing `int.MaxValue` (which previously produced surprisingly large allocations / output) or silently clamping (`--snippet-lines 999999` previously folded down to 20 with no signal). The `--max-line-width` ceiling check is now handled by the same shared upper-bound table. `find` validation rejects out-of-range `--limit` / `--before` / `--after` before reaching the main parser.
- **`--exact`, `--exact-substring`, and `--exact-name` are now mutually exclusive (#1504)** — `QueryCommandRunner` rejects any combination of two or more of the three exactness flags with `Error: pass only one of --exact, --exact-substring, --exact-name.` instead of silently OR-merging them. `search` / `definition` / `references` / `callers` / `callees` / `symbols` / `inspect` all share the same parse-time check, and the CLI `--help` line plus `USER_GUIDE.md` document the constraint so the precedence is no longer undefined.
- **Whitespace-only CLI queries now fail fast with a distinct error (#1505)** — `cdidx search`, `definition`, `references`, `callers`, `callees`, `find`, `inspect`, and `impact` now emit `Error: <command> query cannot be empty or whitespace-only` when the positional query is present but resolves to an empty or whitespace-only value (e.g. `cdidx search "   "`). The original `requires a query argument` message is still used when the query is genuinely missing, so users can distinguish a forgotten argument from a shell-quoting accident before reaching runtime.
- **`cdidx index --rebuild` + `--commits`/`--files` conflict error now shows the valid usage synopses (#1506)** — The conflict-detection `Hint:` line on stderr and the JSON `hint` field now enumerate the three mutually-exclusive forms (`cdidx index <projectPath> --rebuild`, `cdidx index <projectPath> --commits <id> [id ...]`, `cdidx index <projectPath> --files <path> [path ...]`), so users no longer have to re-read `--help` to find the correct command after a flag mistake.
- **Incremental indexing now detects branch / HEAD changes and recommends `--rebuild` (#1508)** — `cdidx index <projectPath>` records the current git HEAD at the end of every successful full scan and compares it on subsequent runs. When the recorded HEAD differs from the workspace HEAD, the CLI emits a `head_changed` warning (also exposed as `head_changed`, `prior_indexed_head_commit`, `current_head_commit`, and `head_change_notice` in `--json` output, and through `workspace_check.head_changed` on `cdidx status --check`) so users know that a default incremental scan after `git switch <branch>` may leave stale rows for branch-specific files. Partial update modes (`--commits` / `--files`) deliberately preserve the captured HEAD so the staleness signal survives until a real full scan reindexes the worktree.
- **`cdidx status --check` no longer reports sparse-checkout-absent files as "missing" (#1510)** — Indexed paths whose git index entry has the skip-worktree bit set (sparse-checkout cone or non-cone, partial clone, or manual `git update-index --skip-worktree`) are now counted under a separate `outside_sparse_cone_file_count` / `outside_sparse_cone_files` bucket and surfaced in the human output as `Outside sparse cone : N`. They no longer fail the freshness gate or push the index toward an unnecessary rebuild, so sparse-checkout monorepos can rely on `--check` for stale-detection again.
- **Warn when the worktree HEAD has switched since the index was built (#1512)** — `status` (CLI text + JSON) and MCP `repo/status` / symbol-analysis responses now compare the persisted `indexed_head_commit` (recorded at the end of a successful full-scan index — see #1508) against the runtime worktree HEAD without needing a `--check` workspace scan. When the two diverge — for example after `git worktree add` or `git switch` inside an existing worktree — the new top-level `indexed_head_commit` / `worktree_head_changed` JSON fields surface the drift, and the CLI text mode prints a `WARN` line with a ready-to-run `cdidx index` command so the index can be refreshed for the current branch. MCP-driven re-indexes mirror the CLI contract and stamp the HEAD only when the run is fully successful, so partial failures keep the prior HEAD and continue to surface staleness until the next clean refresh. Legacy DBs without the persisted HEAD return null fields and trigger no warning, preserving back-compat.
- **Ready-bit stamping no longer drops a flag under concurrent cdidx writers (#1513)** — `SetReadyBit` now wraps its `PRAGMA user_version` read-modify-write in `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` so SQLite serialises it across processes. Previously two parallel `cdidx` runs (e.g. CI plus a local rebuild) could each read the same prior value, OR in their own flag, and the second writer's PRAGMA write would clobber the first writer's flag — leaving `fold_ready` / `graph_table_available` / `issues_table_available` reporting `false` even though both runs had completed successfully.
- **`TryMigrateForRead` now surfaces partial-DDL failures instead of swallowing them (#1516)** — when a schema migration step fails on a restricted mount (read-only filesystem, sandbox, WORM / network share), `DbContext.TryMigrateForRead` now records the failing step, the SQLite error code, and an actionable suggestion on a new `LastMigrationFailure` property, and emits a single `Warning: cdidx schema migration step "<step>" failed (SQLite error <code>: ...)` line to `stderr` pointing the user at writable-storage recovery (e.g. `chmod +w` on the `.cdidx` directory). This replaces the previous broad swallow that left users with cryptic later `no such column` errors and no way to tell whether the cause was permissions, disk space, or corruption.
- **`InsertReferences` no longer leaks orphan `reference_lines` rows when a chunk fails under an outer transaction (#1518)** — the chunked batch writer used to skip its inner transaction when nested under a caller-owned transaction, so a mid-chunk `symbol_references` INSERT failure (e.g. trigger ABORT or FK violation) left the matching `reference_lines` UPSERTs visible to the outer transaction. They were then committed once the outer transaction committed, accumulating as orphan rows that only `--rebuild` could clean. Each batch now opens a chunk-scoped transaction or `SAVEPOINT` so both inserts share a single rollback boundary regardless of outer transaction state.
- **Stop re-evaluating ranking subqueries per FTS hit (#1520)** — `cdidx search` previously embedded two correlated `EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM symbols ...)` subqueries inside the `ORDER BY` of the FTS query. SQLite re-ran each one per result row before applying `--limit`, and the `lower(name) = lower(@q)` / `lower(name) LIKE lower(@prefix)` predicates were not SARGable, so the planner fell back to a full scan of `symbols` — turning a fast FTS hit into an O(N×M) sort on large indexes (most painful for MCP sessions that accumulate many small searches). The two ranking buckets are now sourced from a pair of `LEFT JOIN (SELECT DISTINCT file_id FROM symbols WHERE name = @q COLLATE NOCASE ...)` derived tables that run once per query and reach the existing `idx_symbols_name_nocase` / `idx_symbols_name_folded` indexes, so the observable ranking is unchanged but the query plan no longer carries a correlated scalar subquery.
- **C# base-type resolver no longer silently picks one of multiple `using`-exposed candidates (#1521)** — `DbReader.ResolveScopedCSharpContainingTypeQualifiedName` previously iterated `GetActiveCSharpTypeNamespaces` (a `HashSet<string>` whose enumeration order is non-deterministic) and returned the first namespace whose candidate matched via `FirstOrDefault`. When two `using` directives both exposed a same-named type (`using Foo; using Bar;` both with `Config`), the resolved base for `class Derived : Config` depended on dictionary bucket order, and inheritance walks downstream of `ResolveDirectCSharpBaseContainingTypeScope` could route to whichever candidate happened to enumerate first. The resolver now visits every active namespace, returns the unique match when there is one, and explicitly returns `null` when more than one active namespace exposes a candidate so downstream callers see "ambiguous" rather than a silently-chosen wrong base.
- **Snippet line clamp no longer slices in the middle of a UTF-16 surrogate pair (#1522)** — `LineWidthFormatter.ClampLine` previously substringed by raw UTF-16 code-unit count, so a line ending in a non-BMP character (e.g. 👍 U+1F44D) could leave an orphaned high surrogate at the boundary, rendered as U+FFFD in CLI / JSON `snippet` output. The clamp now backs up by one code unit when the proposed boundary would split a surrogate pair, preserving the full character on both head and focus-window slices.
- **`cdidx --completions fish` now lists `--start` / `--end` for `excerpt`, restoring shell parity (#1525)** — the bash and zsh completion scripts already enumerated every `excerpt` flag, but the fish completion omitted `--start` and `--end`, so fish users typing `cdidx excerpt … --st<Tab>` or `--en<Tab>` got no suggestion even though both flags are required by the documented `cdidx excerpt <path> --start <line> [--end <line>] …` signature. The fish script now emits `complete -c cdidx -n '__fish_seen_subcommand_from excerpt' -l start` / `-l end` entries that mirror the bash/zsh tables, and a new `PrintCompletions_ExcerptFlagSetsMatchAcrossShells` test parses each shell's completion output for the `excerpt` branch and asserts the bash, zsh, and fish flag sets are identical (modulo the universal `--help`), so future shell-completion drift is caught at build time instead of in user reports.
- **Reject overflowing `before` / `after` on the MCP `excerpt` tool (#1528)** — `excerpt` previously fed `before` / `after` straight into `startLine - before` and `endLine + after` without an upper bound, so `before = int.MaxValue` underflowed and an `endLine` near `int.MaxValue` overflowed before `Math.Max` / `Math.Min` could clamp; the slice path then either threw `ArgumentOutOfRangeException` (from `Enumerable.Range` with a negative count) or returned a nonsensical range. The MCP entry now caps `before` / `after` at `[0, 1000]` (mirroring the CLI `--before` / `--after` cap) and reports a clear `"before must be in [0, 1000]"` / `"after must be in [0, 1000]"` error, while `DbReader.GetExcerpt` / `GetExcerptFocusLineLength` and the MCP focus-line bounds check now perform the addition through long intermediates so a huge caller-supplied `endLine` is clamped to the real file length instead of wrapping.
- **Close TOCTOU between `FileInfo.Length` and `ReadAllBytes` in `FileIndexer.BuildRecordWithRawBytes` (#1529)** — `BuildRecordWithRawBytes` previously checked `FileInfo.Length` against `MaxFileSize` (10 MB) and then re-opened the file via `File.ReadAllBytes`, leaving a stat / read window where an auto-generated file (build output, log capture) or an adversarial writer could grow from a small size to multi-GB between the two syscalls and force the indexer into an OOM-sized allocation that bypassed the documented cap. The method now opens the path once through a single `FileStream` (`FileShare.Read`), validates `stream.Length` against `MaxFileSize` on that open handle, and streams the bytes into a `MemoryStream` accumulator whose running total is capped at `MaxFileSize`, so a concurrent appender that keeps writing during the read still throws `InvalidOperationException` (`"File too large (> 10 MB limit; grew during read)"`) instead of allocating beyond the ceiling. `FileRecord.Size` and `FileRecord.Modified` now come from the bytes actually read and the post-open `File.GetLastWriteTimeUtc`, so downstream consumers (status, freshness checks, MCP) see the same payload size the indexer ingested rather than a racy `FileInfo.Length` snapshot.
- **`EnsureColumn` duplicate-column race recovery no longer depends on the English error message (#1532)** — the `ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN` race catch in `DbContext.EnsureColumn` previously keyed on `ex.Message.Contains("duplicate column name")`, so a localized SQLite build or a future wording change would let the exception escape, aborting the migration with a half-applied schema that re-runs could not heal. The catch now re-runs `PRAGMA table_info` and only swallows the `SqliteException` when the column is verifiably present, making the recovery independent of SQLite's error wording.
- **`backfill_fold` no longer stamps `fold_ready=true` when a concurrent writer slipped in a NULL-folded row (#1535)** — `DbWriter.MarkFoldReady` now re-verifies `AllFoldedColumnsBackfilled` inside the same `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` transaction that flips the FoldReady bit and writes `fold_key_version` / `fold_key_fingerprint`, and returns `false` (writing nothing) if the re-verify fails. Previously the MCP `backfill_fold` handler, the `cdidx backfill-fold` CLI, the `cdidx index` full-scan / partial-update restamp paths, and the MCP `index_project` full-scan restamp path all ran the verify and the stamp as separate statements, so a parallel `cdidx index` could insert fresh rows between the two and leave `fold_ready=true` with some `name_folded` / `*_folded` columns still NULL — silently underreporting on `--exact` Unicode searches. All five call sites now surface the same retry-friendly error message they already use for legacy NULL rows, so readers degrade to the NOCASE fallback until `backfill_fold` is re-run successfully.
- **PHP / Rust `use ... as ...` alias parser now anchors on whitespace token boundaries (#1539)** — the PHP group-`use` item parser and the Rust `use`-tree alias parser previously located the ` as ` separator via `IndexOf(" as ")` / `FindTopLevelKeyword(" as ")` and then sliced `item[(aliasIndex + 4)..]` unconditionally, so any whitespace variant other than a single ASCII space (tab-separated `Item\tas\tAlias`, multi-line `Result\n    as\n    IoResult`, or any path that combined tab / newline separators introduced by `TryReadRustUseStatement`) silently failed to match the keyword and the alias was either dropped entirely (PHP, falling back to the full path as the import name) or merged into the leaf name (Rust, the alias never surfaced as its own `import` occurrence). The PHP group-`use` item path now matches the alias via the new `PhpUseGroupItemAliasRegex` (`^(?<name>[\w\\]+)\s+as\s+(?<alias>\w+)$`, case-insensitive), and the Rust path uses a new `FindTopLevelAsKeyword` helper that scans for an ` as ` keyword surrounded by any whitespace (tab / space / newline) outside `{ ... }` and returns the full keyword span so the alias slice is correctly bounded.
- **Detect UTF-16 BOMs and aggregate likely non-UTF8 encodings in `FileIndexer` (#1540)** — `BuildRecordWithRawBytes` previously assumed UTF-8 input and only fell back to a UTF-8 replacement-mode decode when `throwOnInvalidBytes` tripped, so UTF-16 LE/BE source files (BOM `FF FE` / `FE FF`) and BOM-less SHIFT_JIS / GBK / ISO-8859-1 / Latin-1 files were silently mangled into runs of `U+FFFD` and `NUL` characters that hid every symbol they declared. The decoder now BOM-detects UTF-16 LE and UTF-16 BE (excluding the UTF-32 LE prefix `FF FE 00 00`) and decodes those bytes through `UnicodeEncoding` so symbols, references, and search hits stay intact for `cdidx` users on mixed-encoding repos. `ValidateContent` adds two new issue kinds visible from `cdidx validate` and the MCP `validate` tool: `utf16_bom` is emitted exactly once for UTF-16 BOM-decoded files (and suppresses the raw-byte heuristics `bom` / `null_byte` / `mixed_line_endings`, which would otherwise misfire on UTF-16 LE's per-character NUL bytes and `0D 00 0A 00` CRLF), and `non_utf8_likely` collapses a high `U+FFFD` ratio (≥1 % with at least 5 occurrences in the decoded content) into one aggregate issue with the actual count, total chars, and percentage, so a fully mojibake file no longer produces hundreds of near-duplicate `replacement_char` rows that drown the diagnostic. The per-line `replacement_char` emission still runs for low-ratio files so genuine point defects (one stray byte in an otherwise-UTF-8 source) remain actionable, and `cdidx validate --kind utf16_bom` / `--kind non_utf8_likely` now filter the new categories from CLI and MCP.
- **PHPDoc `@property*` tags conflicting on the same `$name` no longer emit duplicate symbols and type references (#1541)** — within a single `/** ... */` docblock, PHP property tags (`@property`, `@property-read`, `@property-write`, plus `@phpstan-property*` / `@psalm-property*` variants) are now deduplicated by property name. The first declaration wins; subsequent tags that re-declare the same `$name` are dropped from both `SymbolExtractor` output and `ReferenceExtractor` `type_reference` edges, so conflicting documentation does not silently inflate `callers` / `impact` results. Distinct docblocks (e.g., two classes in one file) remain independent and emit their own property entries.
- **Indexer no longer stalls on multi-MB single-line files (#1542)** — `ChunkSplitter`, `SymbolExtractor`, and `ReferenceExtractor` now share a 64 KB per-line cap (`ChunkSplitter.MaxLineLength`). When any single physical line exceeds the cap (e.g. a 1 MB minified `*.min.js` bundle or a base64-encoded asset), all three return empty for that file so a multi-MB Content column is not persisted and regex-based extraction cannot stall on catastrophic backtracking / OOM. The skip is surfaced as a `line_too_long` `FileIssue` from `FileIndexer.ValidateContent` so the previously silent stall is now observable through the existing issues channel.
- **CLI numeric flag parsing is now locked to `CultureInfo.InvariantCulture` (#1543)** — `QueryCommandRunner` previously called `int.TryParse(value, out var x)` for `--limit` / `--top` / `--before` / `--after` / `--max-line-width` and for the shared `TryParsePositiveInt` / `TryParseNonNegativeInt` helpers, which leaks the runtime culture's `NumberFormatInfo` (notably `NegativeSign`) into parsing decisions and lets the same `cdidx --limit X` command drift across machines with different locales (or across future locale-aware CLR changes). Every CLI numeric `TryParse` now passes `NumberStyles.Integer, CultureInfo.InvariantCulture` so the parse contract is anchored to ASCII digits and the ASCII `-` sign regardless of `LANG` / `CurrentCulture`.
- **File checksum now normalizes CRLF / CR to LF so cross-OS clones don't trigger spurious full reindex (#1544)** — `FileIndexer.ComputeChecksum` previously hashed the raw on-disk bytes, so the same logical file cloned on Windows (`core.autocrlf=true`, CRLF) and on Linux/macOS (LF) produced different checksums. Teams with mixed-OS developers, or workspaces shared over a NAS, saw `cdidx index` mark every file as "changed" the first time it ran on a different OS, wasting indexing time and poisoning "what files actually changed" diagnostics. The helper now collapses `\r\n` and standalone `\r` to `\n` while streaming through `IncrementalHash` (so a 10 MB file does not need an extra normalized-byte copy), and keeps BOM bytes in the hash input so BOM add / remove still flips the checksum. `DbPathResolver`'s sample-checksum sniffing now calls the same helper so it stays consistent with how the writer recorded the checksum. Added focused regressions for CRLF-vs-LF parity, BOM add/remove detection, mixed-line-ending normalization, and CR-free streaming over multi-chunk inputs; updated the BOM-stripped record test's comment to make the new contract explicit.
- **Stop silently shifting offsetless ISO 8601 timestamps by the local timezone (#1545)** — `DbReader.ParseDateTimeValue` previously parsed string timestamps via `DateTime.TryParse` (which defaults to `AssumeLocal`) and then re-stamped the result as `DateTimeKind.Utc` via `SpecifyKind`, so an offset-bearing column value (e.g. `2025-06-04T15:00:00+09:00`) was first converted to local wall-clock time and then relabeled UTC, drifting `MAX(indexed_at)` / `MAX(modified)` freshness by the reader's local TZ offset. `QueryCommandRunner.TryParseIso8601Since` had the symmetric bug for `--since` inputs: offsetless values were parsed with `DateTimeStyles.AssumeLocal`, so two teammates issuing `--since 2024-01-01T00:00:00` in Tokyo and New York filtered against different logical UTC moments. Both parsers now use `DateTimeStyles.AssumeUniversal | DateTimeStyles.AdjustToUniversal` (DB-side via `DateTime.TryParse` with `CultureInfo.InvariantCulture`; CLI-side via the existing `DateTimeOffset.TryParseExact` path), so offsetless inputs are anchored at UTC and timestamps with an explicit `Z` / `+09:00` offset convert correctly. `USER_GUIDE.md` documents the new convention so callers can opt into a non-UTC interpretation by appending `Z` or an explicit offset.
- **Path case-sensitivity now probes the actual filesystem rather than the host OS family (#1546)** — `PathsEqual` / `IsPathEqualOrParent` previously keyed comparisons on `OperatingSystem.IsWindows()`, so case-sensitive APFS volumes on macOS, case-sensitive WSL NTFS / dev-drives, and case-sensitive ReFS mounts were misclassified (collapsing distinct files like `Foo.cs` vs `foo.cs`, or failing to match equivalent paths on a Windows case-sensitive directory). All four duplicated comparison sites (`DbPathResolver`, `FileIndexer`, `IndexCommandRunner`, `McpToolHandlers`) now delegate to a single `PathCasing` helper that probes by creating a case-variant of an existing ancestor directory, caches the result per anchor, and seeds itself from the workspace `core.ignorecase` + probe at index time. `cdidx status` (human and `--json`) now exposes the resolved case-sensitivity as `path_case_sensitive` so this trust decision is auditable from outside the indexer.
- **Windows long-path support: deep monorepo paths are no longer silently skipped (#1547)** — `FileIndexer` previously called `Directory.EnumerateFiles` / `Directory.EnumerateDirectories` / `File.GetAttributes` / `File.OpenRead` / `new FileStream(...)` / `File.GetLastWriteTimeUtc` / `File.ReadAllLines` with bare absolute paths, and the `cdidx.exe` apphost did not declare itself long-path-aware. On Windows that combination caused any path crossing `MAX_PATH` (260) — typical in `node_modules/.pnpm/<pkg>@<ver>/node_modules/...` trees — to surface as `PathTooLongException` / `DirectoryNotFoundException` and be silently dropped from the index. The fix has three layers that work together: (1) the `cdidx` project now embeds a Windows app manifest with `<longPathAware>true</longPathAware>` so the published apphost honors the OS-level `LongPathsEnabled` registry opt-in; (2) a new `CodeIndex.Indexer.LongPath` helper wraps absolute Windows paths >= 248 characters with the `\\?\` (drive) or `\\?\UNC\` (UNC) extended-length prefix and is a no-op for short / relative / device-namespace / non-Windows / already-prefixed inputs; (3) every walker I/O syscall in `FileIndexer` (`Directory.Enumerate*`, `File.Exists` / `File.GetAttributes` / `File.OpenRead` / `File.ReadLines` / `File.ReadAllLines` / `File.WriteAllText` / `File.Delete` / `new FileStream(...)` / `File.GetLastWriteTimeUtc`, including the `.gitmodules` reader in `LoadGitSubmodulePaths`) plus the downstream `File.Exists` checks that decide which DB rows to keep on every index run (`DbWriter.PurgeStaleFiles`, `IndexCommandRunner` `--update-paths` / `--commits` / per-file update loop) and the `DbPathResolver` checksum sampling are routed through `LongPath.EnsureWindowsPrefix(...)`; entries returned from `Enumerate*` are stripped via `LongPath.RemoveWindowsPrefix(...)` before any `Path.GetRelativePath(_projectRoot, …)` / `NormalizeScopeKey(...)` so canonical project-relative keys stay identical to the short-path case. Without the layer (3) `PurgeStaleFiles` patch, a deep file indexed successfully would have been classified as missing and DELETED from the DB on the next index run, undoing the layer (2) walker fix. POSIX behavior is unchanged because `EnsureWindowsPrefix` short-circuits when `RuntimeInformation.IsOSPlatform(OSPlatform.Windows)` is false; cross-platform unit tests target the internal `EnsureWindowsPrefixCore` / `RemoveWindowsPrefixCore` overloads with an explicit `isWindows` flag so the prefix logic is verified on POSIX CI.
- **MCP server now drains gracefully and bounds in-flight tool calls (#1567)** — the JSON-RPC loop accepts a `notifications/shutdown` notification (and the legacy LSP-style `notifications/exit` alias) that cancels an internal shutdown token, unblocks any pending `IMcpTransport.ReadFrameAsync`, and exits the loop after the current tool call drains; queued frames behind the shutdown notification are no longer served. The loop also acquires a `SemaphoreSlim` (default cap `8`, exposed as `McpServer.MaxConcurrency` and validated by the constructor) before every `ProcessFrame` call so a future parallel-dispatch transport cannot exceed the bound, and the per-request cancellation token is now plumbed into `DbReader` (`new DbReader(connection, isReadOnly, cancellation)` + `DbReader.Cancellation` / `ThrowIfCancellationRequested`) so SQLite work started by a tool observes shutdown and client-disconnect cancellation instead of running to completion after the client is gone. The legacy two-argument `DbReader` constructor is preserved as a thin wrapper that passes `CancellationToken.None`, so existing CLI and test call sites continue to compile unchanged.
- **MCP server now drains cleanly on SIGINT / SIGTERM (#1573)** — `cdidx mcp` (stdio) used to block in `ReadLineAsync` until stdin closed because the loop ran with `CancellationToken.None` and no signal handler, so Ctrl+C / SIGTERM from orchestrators (systemd, launchd, supervisord) hung the process until it was force-killed. The stdio entry point now registers `Console.CancelKeyPress` and `PosixSignal.SIGTERM` against a shared `CancellationTokenSource` and threads the token through the loop, letting the next read return cleanly and the per-session `DbContext` be disposed. The HTTP transport path inherits the same SIGTERM coverage so non-loopback deployments are no longer Ctrl+C-only.
- **`SuggestionStore` no longer leaks `.tmp` files on save failure (#1574)** — `SuggestionStore.SaveUnlocked` previously wrote to `<file>.tmp` and then called `File.Move`, but had no cleanup path if either step threw (e.g. transient filesystem error or anti-virus interference), so repeated failures accumulated orphan `.tmp` files in `.cdidx/`. The write/rename is now wrapped in a `try` / `catch` that best-effort deletes the temp file before rethrowing, keeping the directory free of stale in-progress artifacts.
- **`cdidx index` now absolutizes paths at the option-parsing boundary and surfaces cwd drift (#1577)** — `projectPath` and `--db` are normalized with `Path.GetFullPath` inside `IndexCommandRunner.ParseArgs` (file URIs pass through unchanged), and the runner snapshots `Environment.CurrentDirectory` at startup so it can warn when an embedded host, signal handler, or future plugin changed the cwd by the time the final write happens. Both `index --json` finalize payloads expose `cwd_drift_detected`, `cwd_at_start`, `cwd_at_finalize`, and `cwd_drift_notice` so automation can react to the drift, and the human path prints a matching warning.
- **Hot-path failures now surface a structured Code / Category / Path / Hint instead of a bare `InvalidOperationException` (#1580)** — `CodeIndexException` carries a stable error code, a category, the offending DB or file path, and a recovery hint; CLI prints `Error [Code]: …` plus `Path:` / `Hint:` lines (or a populated `path` / `category` field in `--json` envelopes), and the MCP catch-all echoes `[Code/Category] path='…' hint='…'` so clients can branch on the stable code without parsing message text. The SQLite retry helper now also wraps the final busy attempt instead of rethrowing a raw `SqliteException`, so `cdidx index` against a locked database fails with `E002_DB_LOCKED` and the database path instead of a terse generic message.
